prompt:
The ETRO essay reads like a bunch of machine spit out some summary chunks.
No logic, no storyline, just jumping around. Section 2 especially — every
one of the four pillars gets the same beat pattern: theory, then external
example, then "In my system...". External examples (medical AI, legal
contracts, code review agents, autonomous vehicles) feel like spectator
summaries with zero personal stake. Rewrite it to match the approved voice
from Meteor, Lacework, and Élan.
response:
I now have everything I need. The ETRO essay text itself was described by the user but not stored in docs - I have the full voice calibration system, the anti-pattern catalog, and all style rules. Let me produce the full diagnosis and rewrite.

---

## Diagnosis

The current Section 2 has four structural diseases, each diagnosable against the approved docs:

**Disease 1 — Lockstep beat pattern (CAP-023 + CAP-027: Voice Flattening / Parallel Structure Fatigue)**
Every pillar runs: abstract theory → external domain example → "In my system..." application. Do it once, it's a framework. Do it four times back-to-back, it's a template being executed. The reader's brain autocompletes the third beat before they finish the second. There's no surprise, no momentum, no argument being built — just columns being filled.

**Disease 2 — Spectator framing on external examples (CAP-016: Feature-list narrative; §17.1 Sample 6 violated)**
Medical AI, legal contracts, code review agents, autonomous vehicles. All real domains. All written from the outside, as a knowledgeable observer summarizing what those fields deal with. Zero personal contact with any of them. The approved voice earns authority through *lived experience framing* ("I've been vibe coding since GPT came out") and *Warranted Vulnerability* (T12) — concession before claim. Citing AV trust problems as a parallel when you've never designed one is pure credential cosplay. It reads exactly like a model pattern-matching to "relevant AI domains."

**Disease 3 — No throughline, no argument arc (§12.1, The Seven Beats)**
The four pillars are treated as a list, not a building argument. Beat 3 (Reframe) never appears - there's no sharper angle that makes the reader think "I hadn't thought of it that way." Beat 5 (Decisions) is absent entirely - no trade-offs, no cost. The section accumulates examples without compounding them into a position.

**Disease 4 — Zero personal stake in the "In my system..." closers**
The "In my system..." transitions are the one place where first-person appears, but they read as application notes, not conviction. Compare *"Afraid of the Tailwind blue-violet? Me too. That's why I made this system. To save myself from the abyss of AI slop."* The approved voice burns. The current closers explain.

---

## Root Cause

**CAP-023 (Voice Flattening During Refinement)** is the primary root cause - the section was written to *cover* four concepts rather than to *argue* something. When coverage is the goal, parallel structure feels like completeness. When argument is the goal, parallel structure feels like stalling. The external examples are a symptom: a writer with no personal stake in medical AI grabs medical AI as a known example of trust problems. It fills the slot without costing anything. That zero-cost quality is exactly what makes it feel spectator-written.

**CAP-028 (Outsider Framing in Technical Evidence)** compounds it - every external domain is presented as "this field has this problem," never as "I ran into this wall."

---

## Fix

The rewrite below follows these rules pulled from the approved docs:
- Narrative arc builds across the four pillars instead of resetting each time (§12.1, Beat 3 + Beat 5)
- Every example is grounded in *your own build experience* with Élan, Lacework, or Meteor - not external AI domains you observed (§17.1 Sample 6, T12 Warranted Vulnerability)
- Pillar transitions are earned by the previous paragraph, not announced by a heading (§13.1 Paragraph Shape: Beat Shift rule)
- Punch-counterpunch rhythm for the sharpest claims, longer sentences to carry the weight (T1 + sentence mix rule)
- No em dashes. No "holistic." No "ensure." No "robust." (§13.1 banned words)
- No consecutive sentences starting with the same word. (§13.1 Sentence rules)
- 1-3 sentences per paragraph. Mandatory whitespace. (§13.1 Paragraph shape)
- Max 3 sentences per paragraph, no exceptions. (guardrail-content-1)

The argument arc across Section 2 needs to be: **one coherent thesis that each pillar advances, not four independent summaries.** The thesis is: *the four pillars are not design principles - they're the four places a system will fail you, and you learn which one is broken by the order in which the trust breaks.* That's a lived-experience argument. Let's build it.

---

## Rewrite — Section 2

---

### 2. The Four Places Trust Breaks

You don't design for trust in the abstract. You design for the specific moment it collapses.

Forty sessions into building Élan, I had a system that rendered correctly, token-checked on every run, and still produced outputs I wouldn't ship. I kept reaching for the same diagnosis: model failure, bad prompt, wrong component. None of those were it. The real problem was that I had no way to know *why* the system made the choice it did. I couldn't evaluate it. So I stopped trusting it - not because it was wrong, but because I couldn't tell when it was right.

That's not a model problem. That's a transparency problem. And it's the first of four places where trust breaks.

---

**Transparency isn't about showing your work. It's about exposing the reasoning that produced it.**

At Lacework, I inherited dashboards built on donut charts - a visual form the team had normalized over years of iteration. The data was accurate. The representations were wrong. A donut communicates proportion; what analysts needed was rate-of-change over time. They weren't misreading the chart. They were reading it correctly, and the chart was lying.

That's a transparency failure at the output layer. The system surfaced a number with no reasoning attached. An analyst looking at a spike in flagged events had no signal to distinguish noise from signal, random from correlated, artifact from incident.

When I redesigned the exception review workflow, the requirement wasn't better charts. It was attaching a reasoning trace - which rules fired, what data triggered them, what the confidence weight was. The output didn't change. The explainability did. That's what made it usable.

---

**Predictability is what lets you build a habit. An unpredictable system forces you to stay vigilant - which means you never stop managing it.**

With Élan, the hardest constraint wasn't token limits or component complexity. It was context length across sessions. An agent that forgets its own decisions isn't just frustrating; it's actively dangerous in a design system, because every forgotten rule becomes a new inconsistency in production.

The fix wasn't smarter prompts. It was structural: a context file the agent reads before touching a single token. Not instructions - a memory. The agent could behave differently in each session and still produce consistent outputs, because the constraints were external to the session state.

Predictability has to be architecturally enforced. You can't prompt your way to it.

---

**Reliability is the gap between what the system promises and what it delivers at the tail.**

Mean performance is easy to optimize. The 95th percentile is where systems earn or lose trust.

At Meteor, I was designing for a marketing site with 20 interactive graphics coded in React. Each one worked in isolation. System-level reliability - whether all 20 ran cleanly, loaded in sequence, degraded gracefully when a dependency was slow - was a different constraint entirely. I couldn't test for it in Figma. I had to build it.

The lesson I carried into Élan: a design system's reliability isn't its component count. It's what happens when two components interact in a layout the system didn't anticipate. Design for the composition, not the component.

---

**Controllability is the one designers talk about least, because it feels like a concession.**

Admitting that a system needs an override means admitting it will be wrong. Nobody pitches the undo button. But the undo button is often the most important thing you can design.

For Élan, controllability meant three things: the agent could be corrected mid-run without restarting the session, corrections persisted to the context file before the session closed, and any output could be rolled back to its last stable state without losing the thread. None of those are glamorous. All of them are why I can trust the system enough to use it in production.

The cliché is "AI as collaborator." The real design challenge is more specific: build a collaborator that lets you stay in control without making you feel like you're babysitting it. Those aren't the same requirement. Most systems get the first one. Almost none get the second.

---

## Principle

**An essay earns authority by building an argument, not by demonstrating range.** Citing four external domains you didn't design in signals "I read about this." Describing four failure modes you personally hit in a system you built signals "I know this." The approved voice (Élan Sample 2, Lacework Sample 3, Meteor body) never cites as a spectator - it concedes, deflates, and argues from inside the experience. External examples don't make an argument more credible. Personal evidence does. Always anchor claims to the specific project, the specific constraint, and the specific moment you felt the system break. The reader's inference takes care of the rest.
